questions.question_text: Within how many business days must earnest money be deposited into a trust account after contract acceptance in Maryland?
distractors: 3 business days
5 business days
10 business days
questions.explanation: BOP §17-502(b) requires a broker to deposit trust money promptly, and not more than 7 business days after acceptance of the contract of sale by both parties, in an account kept separate from the broker's own funds and used solely for trust money. Under §17-503 the depository must be a financial institution located in Maryland whose deposits are insured.

questions.question_text: A Maryland broker keeps $500 of personal funds in the brokerage escrow account to cover bank service charges. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, the account must hold trust money only
distractors: Yes, up to $500 is permitted for bank fees
Yes, up to $1,000 is permitted for bank fees
Yes, if the funds are recorded in a separate ledger
questions.explanation: Maryland law does not allow any personal or business funds in a trust account. BOP §17-502(b)(1) requires trust money to be kept in an account maintained separately from the broker's own accounts and used solely for trust money. Keeping personal funds in the account — even to cover bank charges — is commingling and grounds for discipline.

questions.question_text: A Maryland broker wants to place trust funds in an interest-bearing account. What additional requirement applies?
questions.correct_answer: Written agreement from all parties is needed
distractors: MREC must pre-approve the account type
Interest must be donated to a housing fund
Only deposits over $10,000 may earn interest
questions.explanation: In Maryland, a broker may place trust funds in an interest-bearing account only if all parties to the transaction provide written agreement. This ensures all parties consent to how the interest will be handled and distributed.

questions.question_text: Which statement about Maryland trust account requirements is correct?
questions.correct_answer: It must be at an insured institution located in Maryland
distractors: The account may be in any U.S. bank or credit union
Separate accounts are required for each transaction
Online-only banks are prohibited for trust funds
questions.explanation: Under BOP §17-503, trust money must be deposited in a financial institution located in Maryland whose deposits are insured (for example by the FDIC or NCUA). Banks and credit unions both qualify, but the institution must be located in the State — an out-of-state account does not comply. Under COMAR 09.11.01.06 the account must also be clearly designated as containing funds held for others.

questions.question_text: A Maryland trust account label reads 'ABC Realty Business Account.' Does this meet requirements?
questions.correct_answer: No, it must include a trust or escrow designation
distractors: Yes, the brokerage name is sufficient
Yes, if it is at a federally insured bank
No, it must include the MREC license number
questions.explanation: Maryland requires trust accounts to carry a 'trust' or 'escrow' designation in the account name. Labeling it as a 'business account' does not satisfy this requirement, even if the account is at a properly insured Maryland bank.

questions.question_text: How long must a Maryland broker retain trust account records?
distractors: A minimum of 3 years
A minimum of 7 years
Until the broker's license is renewed
questions.explanation: BOP §17-507 requires brokers to keep all records of trust money, listings, and other transaction documents for 5 years, starting on the date of closing — or, if the transaction is not closed, 5 years after the date of the listing. The Commission may inspect records on reasonable notice during business hours, and records of money held in trust must be displayed to MREC on demand.

questions.question_text: MREC arrives at a Maryland brokerage to audit trust account records without prior notice. The broker says the records are on a personal laptop at home. What is the broker's obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Make the electronic records available and readable
distractors: Request a 30-day extension to compile the records
Refuse until MREC obtains a court order
Provide only paper copies within 10 business days
questions.explanation: Records of money held in trust must be displayed to MREC on demand, and electronic records are acceptable if they are produced in readable form — with paper copies at the broker's expense if requested. Note that BOP §17-507 also requires records of trust money to be maintained in a secured area within the broker's office, so keeping them only on a personal laptop at home is itself a compliance problem.

questions.question_text: Which document is NOT typically part of required Maryland trust account records?
questions.correct_answer: Client credit reports and financial statements
distractors: Deposit receipts for all funds received
Bank statements for the escrow account
Records of all disbursements made
questions.explanation: BOP §17-507 requires brokers to keep all records of trust money — including deposit records, disbursement records, and escrow account bank statements — in a secured area within the broker's office for 5 years. Client credit reports and personal financial statements are not trust account records; they pertain to lending rather than escrow management.

questions.question_text: A Maryland broker transfers the firm's escrow account to a different bank. What must the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Notify the Commission in writing within 10 days
distractors: Obtain MREC approval before making the transfer
Notify all parties with funds in escrow within 30 days
Nothing, if the new account is federally insured
questions.explanation: Under COMAR 09.11.01.07, a broker who establishes an additional escrow account, changes the escrow account number, or transfers the account to another bank must notify the Commission in writing within 10 days after the action. Prior MREC approval is not required, but the account must remain an eligible, properly designated escrow account at an insured institution located in Maryland.

questions.question_text: A broker closes their Maryland brokerage. How long after closing must trust account records be retained?
questions.correct_answer: 5 years from the closing of each transaction
distractors: Records may be destroyed upon closing
3 years from the brokerage closing date
1 year after license surrender
questions.explanation: The BOP §17-507 retention duty does not end when a brokerage closes. Each record must be kept for 5 years starting on the date of closing of its transaction — or, if the transaction is not closed, 5 years after the date of the listing — so the former broker must preserve records and remain available for MREC inspection until each record's 5-year period runs.

questions.question_text: A salesperson at a Maryland brokerage closes a transaction without the supervising broker reviewing any documents. Who bears responsibility for compliance failures?
distractors: Only the salesperson who handled the deal
The buyer's agent in the transaction
MREC holds no one accountable in this case
questions.explanation: In Maryland, the supervising broker is responsible for the acts of all affiliated licensees. The broker must ensure compliance with real estate laws and review and maintain all transaction records. Failure to supervise can result in disciplinary action against the broker.

questions.question_text: What is the role of a branch office manager in Maryland's broker supervision structure?
questions.correct_answer: Acts on behalf of the supervising broker
distractors: Operates independently of the main broker
Has no supervisory authority over agents
Must hold a separate brokerage license
questions.explanation: In Maryland, branch office managers act on behalf of the supervising broker. They help ensure compliance, oversee day-to-day operations, and maintain transaction records at their location, but ultimate responsibility remains with the supervising broker.

questions.question_text: A Maryland brokerage's unlicensed assistant accompanies a licensee to a showing. While the licensee greets the buyer, the assistant begins describing the home's upgraded kitchen and other selling points. Which action is prohibited?
questions.correct_answer: Discussing the property's selling points with the buyer
distractors: Scheduling the showing appointment
Accompanying the licensee to the showing
Handing out preprinted property flyers
questions.explanation: MREC's guidelines for unlicensed employees prohibit discussing the attributes or amenities of a property, under any circumstances, with a prospective purchaser. An unlicensed assistant may schedule appointments for a licensee to show listed property, accompany a licensee to a showing or open house for security purposes, and hand out preprinted materials — but anything that amounts to showing property or discussing its features requires a license.

questions.question_text: An unlicensed assistant at a Maryland brokerage receives a call from a prospective buyer asking about the price reduction terms on a listed property. What should the assistant do?
questions.correct_answer: Take a message and have a licensed agent call back
distractors: Provide the current listing price and terms
Explain the price reduction details from the MLS
Email the buyer the listing agreement summary
questions.explanation: Unlicensed assistants cannot discuss contract terms, pricing negotiations, or listing details with clients or prospective buyers. The appropriate action is to take a message and have a licensed agent return the call to discuss any substantive property or transaction information.

questions.question_text: Which task may an unlicensed assistant legally perform at a Maryland brokerage?
distractors: Negotiating repair credits with the buyer
Hosting an open house without an agent present
Advising a client on the contract contingencies
questions.explanation: Under MREC's guidelines, unlicensed assistants may prepare flyers and promotional materials subject to review and approval by the licensee and supervising broker, and may hand out preprinted materials. They cannot negotiate, host an open house or show property, or advise clients on contracts — those activities require a license.
